title: Fast Power (Exponentiation by Squaring)
code: public static double MyPow(double x, int n)

frames.caption: Widen the exponent: e = n = 13. The bits of 13 are 1101; we will read them low → high.
frames.note: Negative exponents reuse the same machine: x^-n = (1/x)^n, so flip x to its reciprocal and make the exponent positive. e is widened to long before negating because -int.MinValue overflows a 32-bit int — a classic edge-case guard. For 3^13 the exponent is already positive, so this branch is skipped.

frames.caption: e = 13 ≥ 0, so x stays 3 and e stays 13. (For negative n we would set x = 1/x and e = -e.)
frames.note: Negative exponents reuse the same machine: x^-n = (1/x)^n, so flip x to its reciprocal and make the exponent positive. e is widened to long before negating because -int.MinValue overflows a 32-bit int — a classic edge-case guard. For 3^13 the exponent is already positive, so this branch is skipped.

frames.caption: Initialize result = 1 (multiplicative identity) and baseVal = x = 3. baseVal will hold x^(2^step).
frames.note: The whole trick is x^n = (x^(n/2))² for even n, and x·(x^((n-1)/2))² for odd n. Reading the exponent bottom-up in binary, each step squares the running base (so it represents x^(2^step)) and halves the exponent. That is one squaring per exponent bit — O(log n) multiplications instead of the n you would do by multiplying x into the answer one factor at a time.

frames.caption: Loop: e = 13 > 0. Low bit = 1. Current power baseVal = 3 = 3^1.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Bit 0 is 1 → fold this power in: result *= 3 = 3.
frames.note: The current low bit of the exponent decides whether this squared base belongs in the answer. Bit set (odd exponent) -> fold it in with result *= baseVal; bit clear -> skip it, but still square the base for the next position. Folding in only on set bits is exactly reconstructing n from its binary digits 1101.

frames.caption: Square the base for the next bit: baseVal = 3 × 3 = 9 = 3^2.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Halve the exponent: e = 13 >> 1 = 6. Consumed bit 0; move to the next position.
frames.note: e >>= 1 drops the bit we just consumed and slides to the next one — the divide-and-conquer halving of the exponent. The recurrence is T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), which unrolls to O(log n). When e reaches 0 every bit has been processed and the loop ends.

frames.caption: Loop: e = 6 > 0. Low bit = 0. Current power baseVal = 9 = 3^2.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Bit 1 is 0 → skip. This power is not part of 3^13, so result stays 3.
frames.note: The current low bit of the exponent decides whether this squared base belongs in the answer. Bit set (odd exponent) -> fold it in with result *= baseVal; bit clear -> skip it, but still square the base for the next position. Folding in only on set bits is exactly reconstructing n from its binary digits 1101.

frames.caption: Square the base for the next bit: baseVal = 9 × 9 = 81 = 3^4.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Halve the exponent: e = 6 >> 1 = 3. Consumed bit 1; move to the next position.
frames.note: e >>= 1 drops the bit we just consumed and slides to the next one — the divide-and-conquer halving of the exponent. The recurrence is T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), which unrolls to O(log n). When e reaches 0 every bit has been processed and the loop ends.

frames.caption: Loop: e = 3 > 0. Low bit = 1. Current power baseVal = 81 = 3^4.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Bit 2 is 1 → fold this power in: result *= 81 = 243.
frames.note: The current low bit of the exponent decides whether this squared base belongs in the answer. Bit set (odd exponent) -> fold it in with result *= baseVal; bit clear -> skip it, but still square the base for the next position. Folding in only on set bits is exactly reconstructing n from its binary digits 1101.

frames.caption: Square the base for the next bit: baseVal = 81 × 81 = 6561 = 3^8.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Halve the exponent: e = 3 >> 1 = 1. Consumed bit 2; move to the next position.
frames.note: e >>= 1 drops the bit we just consumed and slides to the next one — the divide-and-conquer halving of the exponent. The recurrence is T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), which unrolls to O(log n). When e reaches 0 every bit has been processed and the loop ends.

frames.caption: Loop: e = 1 > 0. Low bit = 1. Current power baseVal = 6561 = 3^8.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Bit 3 is 1 → fold this power in: result *= 6561 = 1594323.
frames.note: The current low bit of the exponent decides whether this squared base belongs in the answer. Bit set (odd exponent) -> fold it in with result *= baseVal; bit clear -> skip it, but still square the base for the next position. Folding in only on set bits is exactly reconstructing n from its binary digits 1101.

frames.caption: Square the base for the next bit: baseVal = 6561 × 6561 = 43046721 = 3^16.
frames.note: Squaring is what turns linear into logarithmic. After step k the base equals x^(2^k): 3 -> 9 -> 81 -> 6561 = 3^1, 3^2, 3^4, 3^8. Doubling the exponent of the base each step means a handful of squarings already covers a huge true exponent, which is the practical meaning of O(log n).

frames.caption: Halve the exponent: e = 1 >> 1 = 0. Consumed bit 3; move to the next position.
frames.note: e >>= 1 drops the bit we just consumed and slides to the next one — the divide-and-conquer halving of the exponent. The recurrence is T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), which unrolls to O(log n). When e reaches 0 every bit has been processed and the loop ends.

frames.caption: e = 0 — every bit processed. Return result = 1594323. So 3^13 = 3 × 81 × 6561 = 1594323 in just 4 squarings, O(log n).
frames.note: Four steps replaced thirteen naive multiplications; for a million-sized exponent it would be ~20 steps instead of a million. Halving the exponent each round is the divide-and-conquer that makes Pow O(log n) time and O(1) space.
